---
title: "Separating chromosomes in fused cells"
---

[← Back to research](../../research.qmd){.back-link}

[co-author (second author) · Communications Biology, 2022]{.paper-meta}

:::: {.columns .vmiddle}
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](figure1.jpg){.paper-figure style="width:100%; max-width:100%; margin:0"}

[Figure 1 reproduced from Sunchu B., Lee N. M., Taylor J. A., Segura R. C., Roubinet C. &amp;
Cabernard C. (2022). *Asymmetric chromatin retention and nuclear envelopes separate
chromosomes in fused cells in vivo.* **Communications Biology** 5, 953.
[doi:10.1038/s42003-022-03874-z](https://doi.org/10.1038/s42003-022-03874-z). © 2022 The
Author(s). Licensed under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).]{.figure-credit style="max-width:100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="4%"}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
Every cell division must distribute its replicated chromosomes equally to two daughter cells,
and most metazoan cells do so with a single bipolar spindle. But a cell can end up carrying
two distinct genomes at fertilization, or through cell fusion, when two cells merge into
a hybrid, and it must then keep chromosomes of different origins physically apart and segregate
them faithfully; failure leads to chromosome missegregation and aneuploidy. Over a century ago,
Aichel proposed that unregulated somatic cell fusion could initiate tumors, since the resulting
tetraploidy and extra centrosomes predispose cells to aneuploidy. How a hybrid cell keeps two
genomes apart inside a living animal had not been characterized.
:::
::::

To watch it happen, we built a hybrid-cell model in the *Drosophila* larval brain: a brief
pulse from a 532 nm laser fuses a neural stem cell (a neuroblast) with one of its smaller,
differentiating daughters, turning the fused cell into a live stage for following two genomes at
once.

The **timing of fusion strongly shaped the outcome**. Fusions induced in interphase or early
prophase let the hybrid independently align both the endogenous and ectopic chromosomes at the
metaphase plate, each on a spindle built from its own centrosomes; fusions in metaphase or
anaphase usually left the incoming chromatin unable to align. The two chromosome sets could even
enter anaphase asynchronously, hinting at a distance-dependent "wait-anaphase" checkpoint signal.

What keeps the two genomes from intermingling? **Two cooperating mechanisms.** First, asymmetric,
microtubule-dependent chromatin retention: the neuroblast maintains a single active apical
microtubule-organizing center (MTOC) that holds its own centromeres near the cortex, away from
the incoming chromatin — disrupting this interphase bias let the two centromere pools converge far
earlier than in controls. Second, separate nuclear envelopes: each chromatin pool stays enclosed
within its own envelope, a physical barrier that separates the genomes until roughly metaphase.
Segregation was error-prone — lagging chromosomes, anaphase bridges and micronuclei — consistent
with Aichel's century-old cancer-by-fusion hypothesis. We found no evidence of active chromosome
"recognition"; asymmetric interphase centrosome activity together with nuclear envelopes are
sufficient to explain genome separation, a mechanism that may also operate at the first zygotic
division.

As the paper's second author, I laid much of the groundwork: through extensive troubleshooting I
helped establish the laser-fusion system and uncovered much of the timing-dependent behavior
at its heart, and I built the foundation for the processing and analysis of the imaging data
behind these results.

To learn more: [10.1038/s42003-022-03874-z](https://doi.org/10.1038/s42003-022-03874-z)

[]{.section-rule}
